title: Chapter 3: First Glitches
content: <!-- Speed: Breather | D7+D12 -->
The subway camera didn't beep.

It should have. It always did — that flat electronic chirp when a passenger crossed the threshold at the turnstile, the screen above flashing a scrambled half-image of the person passing through. Every morning for two years, Wren had heard that beep. It was part of the texture of the commute, part of the noise she muted with her headphones and the noise she couldn't mute with anything.
Today: nothing.

She pushed through the turnstile. The metal bar gave way as usual. Behind her, a man in a suit tapped his card and the camera beeped for him — bright, indifferent, mechanical. Wren glanced up at the screen above the gate. It cycled through:Suit Man, sharp and clear. The feed before him — a woman with a stroller, a teenager with a skateboard.
No Wren.

She stood on the platform and told herself it was a glitch. A camera glitch. These things happened all the time. The MTA wasn't exactly running state-of-the-art surveillance. Probably just a frame drop. Probably nothing.

The train came. She got on. Found a seat by the door — always by the door, always an exit plan. The car was half-full, the morning crowd still thin at seven-fifteen. She pulled her hoodie tighter and let the train's rhythm rock her.
She felt good.

That was the strange part. She actually felt — not great, not transformed, not the way the internet testimonials for experimental drugs described euphoria or clarity or the lifting of a veil. Just... good. Quiet. The knot in her chest she'd noticed loosening in the parking lot was still loose. Her hands weren't shaking. Her jaw wasn't clenched. Usually by now — second stop, somewhere between Bedford and Lorimer — she'd have catalogued every person in the car, assessed threat levels, mapped the exits, rehearsed the social cost of making eye contact with a stranger.
Today she just sat.
It was weird. Good-weird. The kind of weird you don't question because questioning it might make it stop.

At Café Dorit, her usual spot, the barista — whose name tag read ELI, who had served Wren an oat-milk latte every weekday morning for eight months — looked right at her, blinked, and turned to the man behind her in line.
"What can I get you?"
Wren stood there. The man ordered. Eli made the drink. The man paid and left. The line moved.
"Next?"

Eli was looking past her. Not through her — not like she was a ghost or a special effect. Just past, the way you look past a fire hydrant or a parking meter. She was in the frame but not in the focus.
"Um." Wren's voice came out small. "Oat latte. Please."
Eli startled. Just a tiny flinch — shoulders up half an inch, a sharp intake of breath. Then his face reset to professional neutral. "Sorry — didn't see you there. Oat latte. Got it."

Didn't see you there. Wren paid. She took her drink to the corner table, the one by the window where the light was bad for reading but good for hiding. Her hands were steady around the cup. The latte was the same as always — nutty, too hot, a little over-foamed.
She sipped it and thought: this is fine.
This is actually fine.

The walk from the café to her apartment was twelve blocks. She knew every crack in the sidewalk, every awning that leaked in the rain, every store where the door stuck. She'd walked this route thousands of times, always with her headphones on, always with her shoulders up, always bracing for the moment when a stranger would bump her or ask her for directions or stand too close on the sidewalk.
Today was different.

She walked through the morning crowd like a warm knife through butter. Bodies parted around her — not dramatically, not like the Red Sea, just a natural drift. Shoulders turned away. Gazes slid off. She was a stone in a stream and the water found a way around her without even knowing she was there.
At the corner of Fifth and Main, a woman with a stroller stopped and let Wren pass. Wren hadn't been in the way. The woman just... adjusted. Stepped slightly to the left. Kept walking.
Wren stopped at a crosswalk and caught her reflection in the window of the pharmacy.
Or tried to.

The window showed the street behind her — the blue van, the man with the dog, the girl on the phone. It showed the awning of the coffee shop across the way. It showed sky, washed-out white, threatening rain again.
It did not show Wren.

She looked down at herself. Hands, check. Feet, check. The hem of her hoodie hanging past her hips, the toe of her left boot where the sole was starting to separate. All present. All physical. All undeniably real.
She looked back at the window. The reflection of the girl on the phone adjusted her hair.
No Wren.

A chill ran across the back of her neck — not fear, not yet, just the uncanny tickle of a thing not computing. She stepped closer to the glass. The window was clean. No distortion, no smudge where her reflection should be. Just — absence. Like she'd been Photoshopped out of the world's image.

Then the crosswalk signal changed, and someone bumped her elbow — actually made contact — and jumped like they'd touched a live wire. A guy in a Giants jersey. He looked at his own arm, then around, then shrugged and kept walking.
Wren crossed the street.

At the next block, the automatic door of the bodega hesitated. She stood in front of it and it didn't open. She stood there for two full seconds — long enough to notice, long enough to feel the wrongness of it — and then it slid open halfway, reluctantly, like it was doing her a favor.
She bought a banana and a bottle of water. The cashier rang her up without looking at her face. That part was normal.
Walking home, she passed three more shop windows. She didn't check them. She wasn't ready to check them.
In her apartment, she stood in the bathroom and looked in the mirror.
Her reflection was there. Tired, hoodie-washed-out, dark circles like bruises under her eyes — all there. The bathroom mirror was old and cheap and had no reason to lie.
She exhaled. Her breath fogged the glass.
"It was just the store windows," she said to the empty bathroom. "Anti-glare coating. Something."
She didn't believe it. She didn't not believe it. She was in the space between — the comfortable, warm, well-furnished space between knowing something was wrong and having to deal with it.
Her phone buzzed. Kayla: U left ur mug at the café. Eli says he'll hold it.
Wren stared at the text. She hadn't left a mug. She'd taken her latte in a paper cup, like always. Eli had served her — he'd seen her — he'd —
She typed: wrong number
Kayla: ??? girl its me

Wren put the phone down. She sat on the couch and pulled her knees up and let the quiet settle around her. The apartment was still. No noise except the hum of the refrigerator and the distant clatter of a neighbor's dishes.
She felt calm.

That was the thing. Underneath the small wrong observations — the camera, the window, the door, the reflections that weren't — she felt a calm so deep it was almost narcotic. The anxiety was still there, she could sense it at the edges, but it was muffled. Like listening to traffic from inside a building with thick walls. The noise was still out there. She just couldn't touch it, and it couldn't touch her.
ANX-00 was working.

She pulled out her sketchbook and opened to a blank page. Her hand moved without hesitation — a woman standing in a crowd, shoulders down, face calm. No eyes visible. Just the outline of a person at peace in a world that didn't know she existed.
She drew for an hour. It was the best hour of drawing she'd had in months.

That afternoon, she walked to the park. Not a destination — just movement, just the need to be outside in the open air where she could test the feeling without walls around it. The park was busy for a Tuesday: joggers, dog-walkers, a scatter of teenagers on the basketball court, a man selling mango slices from a cart.
Nobody looked at her.

She walked through a knot of tourists clustered around a map and they shifted around her without seeming to notice they'd shifted. A jogger veered six inches to avoid her and kept running. A child pointed at a squirrel and his finger passed through the space where her shadow should have been.
She didn't have a shadow.

She noticed it in chunks. First the absence of the dark shape on the concrete. Then the realization that the sun was behind her, low and warm, and should have thrown her silhouette long across the path. Then the absence itself — the nothing where something should be.
Okay.
She sat on a bench. A woman at the other end was reading a paperback. She didn't glance at Wren. She didn't stiffen at the proximity of a stranger. She just read, comfortable, as if she were alone.
Wren closed her eyes. Behind the lids, nothing red or panicked. Just dark. Just rest.
She thought: this is what it feels like to be nobody.
She thought: this is the best I've felt in years.

The two thoughts sat next to each other, and the gap between them — the gap between erasure and peace — was thinner than she wanted to examine. So she didn't. She just sat on the bench in the park in the last warm light of the afternoon and let herself be unseen, and it felt like flying.

She walked home through the crowd and nobody touched her, and when she got back to her apartment she locked the door and took the second pill from the amber bottle without hesitating, and she slept for nine hours straight, which was a personal record, and she did not dream at all.
